LabDataset#

LabDataset abstraction for handling lab experiments data in urban water systems.

This module defines the ‘LabDataset’ class used to handle lab experiments measurements in the OpenWaterLab (OWL) ecosystem.

The LabDataset class extends the Dataset class by providing additional functionalities for using lab data for validation purposes.

Classes#

LabDataset

Extends Dataset class for lab experiments data measurements.

class owl.data.lab_dataset.LabDataset(data, *, timedata_column='index', data_type='WWTP', experiment_tag='No tag given', time_unit=None, units=None, meta=None, id_column=None)[source]#

Bases: Dataset

Dataset class for handling laboratory and experimental data.

This class extends the base Dataset with functionality specific to laboratory measurements, including support for metadata, sample identifiers, and quality control annotations.

Parameters:

  • data (DataFrame) – Input dataset containing laboratory measurements.

  • timedata_column (str) – Column used as the time reference, or "index" if the index represents time.

  • data_type (str) – Type of data represented in the dataset.

  • experiment_tag (str) – Label identifying the experiment or dataset.

  • time_unit (str | None) – Unit of time used for the dataset (e.g., "s", "min", "h").

  • units (Dict[str, str] | None) – Mapping of column names to their corresponding units.

  • meta (DataFrame | None) – Metadata associated with each data point, aligned with the dataset index.

  • id_column (str | None) – Name of the column containing sample or experiment identifiers.

meta#

Metadata associated with the dataset.

Type:

pandas.DataFrame

id_column#

Column used to identify samples or experiments, if available.

Type:

str or None

meta_qc#

DataFrame storing quality control information for the dataset.

Type:

pandas.DataFrame

Notes

  • If meta is not provided, an empty DataFrame is initialized with the same index as the data.

  • The id_column is only assigned if it exists in the dataset.

  • The class is designed for laboratory workflows where additional metadata and quality control tracking are required.

align_with_reference(analyte, reference, *, method='nearest', tolerance=Timedelta('0 days 00:30:00'))[source]#

Align laboratory measurements with a reference time series.

The method matches discrete laboratory measurements to a continuous reference (e.g., sensor or model data) based on timestamps.

Parameters:

  • analyte (str) – Name of the laboratory data column to align.

  • reference (Series | DataFrame) – Reference time series to align with the laboratory data.

  • method (Literal['nearest', 'linear', 'ffill']) – Method used to align or interpolate the reference values to the laboratory timestamps.

  • tolerance (Timedelta | None) – Maximum allowed time difference for matching values when using method="nearest". If exceeded, no match is assigned.

Returns:

DataFrame indexed by laboratory timestamps with columns ["lab", "ref"].

Return type:

DataFrame

Notes

  • The laboratory data is assumed to be discrete, while the reference is

continuous or higher-frequency. - The alignment is performed on the laboratory timestamps. - The chosen method determines how reference values are interpolated or matched.

apply_calibration(reference, slope, intercept=0.0, *, name=None)[source]#

Apply a linear calibration model to reference data.

The method computes calibrated values using a linear relationship of the form lab = slope * reference + intercept.

Parameters:

  • reference (Series | DataFrame) – Reference time series used to generate calibrated values.

  • slope (float) – Slope of the calibration model.

  • intercept (float) – Intercept of the calibration model.

  • name (str | None) – Name assigned to the resulting calibrated series.

Returns:

Calibrated values indexed by the reference timestamps.

Return type:

Series

Notes

  • The calibration is applied directly to the reference data.

  • The output is aligned with the reference index.

  • The dataset itself is not modified.

apply_dilutions_and_units(analytes, *, dilution_factors=1.0, unit_map=None)[source]#

Apply dilution factors and unit conversions to selected analytes.

The method adjusts measured values based on specified dilution factors and optionally converts them to target units using provided conversion factors.

Parameters:

  • analytes (Iterable[str]) – Names of the columns (analytes) to process.

  • dilution_factors (float | Dict[str, float]) – Dilution factors applied to the selected analytes. If a single value is provided, it is applied to all analytes. If a dictionary is provided, factors are applied per analyte.

  • unit_map (Dict[str, Tuple[str, float]] | None) – Mapping of analyte names to target units and conversion factors. Each entry should be of the form {column: (unit, factor)}, where factor is multiplied with the data to convert units.

Return type:

None

Notes

  • Dilution factors are applied before unit conversions.

  • Unit conversions are performed by multiplying values with the provided

conversion factor. - The dataset is modified in place.

compute_validation_metrics(pairs, *, bias_as='ref_minus_lab', zero_guard=1e-12)[source]#

Compute validation metrics between laboratory and reference data.

The method evaluates common statistical metrics based on paired values of laboratory measurements and reference data.

Parameters:

  • pairs (DataFrame) – DataFrame containing paired values with columns ["lab", "ref"].

  • bias_as (Literal['ref_minus_lab', 'lab_minus_ref']) – Definition of bias used in the calculations.

  • zero_guard (float) – Small value used to avoid division by zero in metric computations.

Returns:

Object containing the computed validation metrics.

Return type:

ValidationMetrics

Notes

  • Metrics may include bias, RMSE, MAE, and related statistics.

  • The definition of bias depends on the bias_as parameter.

  • The method assumes that the input data is already aligned.

control_chart(analyte, *, subgroup=None, window=20)[source]#

Compute Shewhart X-bar control chart statistics.

The method calculates the mean and control limits (±3 standard deviations) either over a rolling window or within defined subgroups.

Parameters:

  • analyte (str) – Name of the column to analyze.

  • subgroup (str | None) – Column used to define subgroups (e.g., batches). If provided, statistics are computed per subgroup instead of using a rolling window.

  • window (int) – Size of the rolling window used when subgroup is not provided.

Returns:

DataFrame containing the computed mean and control limits, suitable for plotting a control chart.

Return type:

DataFrame

Notes

  • Control limits are defined as mean ± 3 * standard deviation.

  • If subgroup is provided, statistics are computed within each subgroup.

  • The method is intended for quality control and monitoring applications.

fit_calibration(analyte, reference, *, align_method='nearest', tolerance=Timedelta('0 days 00:30:00'), zero_intercept=False)[source]#

Fit a linear calibration model between laboratory and reference data.

The method aligns laboratory measurements with a reference time series and fits a linear model of the form lab = a * ref + b.

Parameters:

  • analyte (str) – Name of the laboratory data column to calibrate.

  • reference (Series | DataFrame) – Reference time series used for calibration.

  • align_method (Literal['nearest', 'linear', 'ffill']) – Method used to align or interpolate the reference values to the laboratory timestamps.

  • tolerance (Timedelta | None) – Maximum allowed time difference for matching values when using align_method="nearest".

  • zero_intercept (bool) – If True, fit the model with zero intercept (b = 0).

Returns:

Dictionary containing the fitted parameters with keys {"slope", "intercept", "r2"}.

Return type:

Dict[str, float]

Notes

  • Alignment is performed prior to fitting the calibration model.

  • The model is fitted using paired values of laboratory and reference data.

  • The coefficient of determination (R²) is included as a measure of fit quality.

  • The dataset itself is not modified.

rolling_validation(analyte, reference, *, window=20, step=5, align_method='nearest', tolerance=Timedelta('0 days 00:30:00'))[source]#

Perform rolling validation between laboratory and reference data.

The method evaluates validation metrics over successive windows to monitor temporal changes or drift in the relationship between laboratory and reference measurements.

Parameters:

  • analyte (str) – Name of the laboratory data column to validate.

  • reference (Series | DataFrame) – Reference time series used for validation.

  • window (int) – Number of samples in each rolling window.

  • step (int) – Step size between consecutive windows.

  • align_method (Literal['nearest', 'linear', 'ffill']) – Method used to align or interpolate the reference values to the laboratory timestamps.

  • tolerance (Timedelta | None) – Maximum allowed time difference for matching values when using align_method="nearest".

Returns:

DataFrame indexed by window end points containing validation metrics for each window.

Return type:

DataFrame

Notes

  • Alignment is performed prior to computing validation metrics in each window.

  • The method is useful for detecting drift or time-varying performance.

  • The dataset itself is not modified.

validate_against(analyte, reference, *, align_method='nearest', tolerance=Timedelta('0 days 00:30:00'), bias_as='ref_minus_lab', return_pairs=False)[source]#

Validate laboratory data against a reference time series.

The method aligns laboratory measurements with a reference (e.g., sensor or model data) and computes validation metrics based on the aligned pairs.

Parameters:

  • analyte (str) – Name of the laboratory data column to validate.

  • reference (Series | DataFrame) – Reference time series used for validation.

  • align_method (Literal['nearest', 'linear', 'ffill']) – Method used to align or interpolate the reference values to the laboratory timestamps.

  • tolerance (Timedelta | None) – Maximum allowed time difference for matching values when using align_method="nearest".

  • bias_as (Literal['ref_minus_lab', 'lab_minus_ref']) – Definition of bias used in the validation metrics.

  • return_pairs (bool) – If True, also return the aligned data pairs used for validation.

Returns:

Computed validation metrics. If return_pairs=True, also returns the DataFrame of aligned pairs with columns ["lab", "ref"].

Return type:

ValidationMetrics | Tuple[ValidationMetrics, DataFrame]

Notes

  • Alignment is performed using the specified method before computing metrics.

  • The method combines alignment and metric computation into a single workflow.

  • The dataset itself is not modified.

class owl.data.lab_dataset.ValidationMetrics(n, mae, rmse, mbe, mape, r, r2, ccc, slope, intercept)[source]#

Bases: object

ccc: float#
intercept: float#
mae: float#
mape: float#
mbe: float#
n: int#
r: float#
r2: float#
rmse: float#
slope: float#
